ME.intro: Montenegro is one of the most keenly priced coastal markets on the Adriatic: it uses the euro, and foreign buyers may purchase an apartment or a house on effectively the same terms as locals. Property transfer tax has been banded since 2024: 3% up to €150,000, 5% above that, and 6% above €500,000. Demand is concentrated in Budva, Kotor, Tivat (Porto Montenegro) and Herceg Novi; sea-view apartments in the Bay of Kotor are the most sought-after investment product, while Bar and Ulcinj still offer a materially cheaper entry point. The country is an EU candidate, which is a long-term upward driver of prices.
highlights: The euro is the official currency — no exchange-rate risk on the purchase price
Banded transfer tax since 2024: 3% / 5% / 6% (thresholds at €150,000 and €500,000)
No transfer tax on new-build — 21% VAT is included in the price instead
Foreign individuals may freely buy apartments, houses and building plots
There is NO value threshold for EU citizens seeking residence (€150,000 for third-country nationals since 2026)
EU candidate country — long-term appreciation potential

faq.q: Can a foreigner buy real estate in Montenegro?
faq.a: Yes. A foreign private individual may freely buy a flat, house and building plot in Montenegro, in their own name, without a special permit. Restrictions apply primarily to agricultural and forest land — these are usually bought through a local company.

faq.q: What are the ancillary costs of buying real estate in Montenegro?
faq.a: The transfer tax has been banded since 2024: up to 150 000 € it is 3%; between 150 000 and 500 000 € it is 4 500 € plus 5% of the part above 150 000 €; above 500 000 € it is 22 000 € plus 6% of the part above 500 000 €. Its base is the market value determined by the tax authority, not necessarily the contract price. Added to this are the notary fee, roughly 1% lawyer's fee, and the agent's commission (typically 3%, customarily paid by the seller). On the first sale of a newly built property constructed after 2003 there is no transfer tax — there is 21% VAT in the price instead.

faq.q: Does Montenegro grant a residence permit for buying real estate?
faq.a: Yes, on the basis of property ownership a one-year residence permit, renewable annually, can be applied for. From 17 January 2026 a third-country citizen needs property with a tax-authority value of at least 150 000 € for this — but EU, EEA and Swiss citizens ARE NOT SUBJECT TO THIS VALUE LIMIT, so a Hungarian buyer has no minimum. The former citizenship-by-investment programme closed on 31 December 2022, so today you cannot obtain citizenship through real estate in Montenegro — through real estate a route is offered primarily by Turkey.

HR.intro: Croatia is the mature, EU-member market of the Adriatic: since 2023 it has been part of both the eurozone and the Schengen area, which makes it the lowest-friction coastal purchase available to Hungarian and other EU buyers. As an individual, an EU citizen buys on the same terms as locals — the ministerial consent formerly required of foreigners is no longer needed. Istria (Rovinj, Poreč) drives the near-town segment, Dalmatia (Split, Trogir, Šibenik) the holiday-home segment, and Dubrovnik the premium end. Yields from tourist letting are high because of seasonality, but heavily concentrated in the summer.
highlights: EU and eurozone member state — no consent requirement for EU citizens
Property transfer tax is a flat 3%; on new-build, 25% VAT is included in the price instead
Low notary costs: the notary only certifies signatures (a few euros) and does not draft the contract
Since 2025 there is also an ANNUAL property tax: €0.60–8.00 per m² per year, set by each municipality
The moratorium on agricultural land expired on 30 June 2023 — EU buyers may now purchase freely
A strong but highly seasonal short-term letting market

faq.q: Can a Hungarian citizen buy property in Croatia?
faq.a: Yes, without restriction. As both Hungary and Croatia are EU member states, a Hungarian individual may buy an apartment or house in their own name on the same terms as Croatian citizens — no consent from the Ministry of Justice is required. The transitional moratorium on agricultural land expired on 30 June 2023 and was not extended, so as an EU citizen you may now also buy farmland on the same terms as Croatians. Third-country buyers still require reciprocity and ministerial consent.

faq.q: How much tax is payable on a Croatian property purchase?
faq.a: On a second-hand property the buyer pays 3% property transfer tax (porez na promet nekretnina) — a flat rate, not banded. On a new-build bought from a VAT-registered developer there is no transfer tax, because the price includes 25% VAT; the two are mutually exclusive. Importantly, the Croatian notary does NOT draft the sale contract, but merely certifies the seller's signature, so notary costs amount to a few euros plus the land registry fee — the '1% notary fee' customary elsewhere is simply wrong here. Legal fees are typically 1–1.5% plus VAT, and agency commission 2–4%. Since 2025 ownership also attracts an annual property tax (€0.60–8.00 per m² per year), from which registered permanent residence and long-term letting of at least 10 months are exempt.

faq.q: Is it worth buying property in Croatia for short-term letting?
faq.a: On the Dalmatian and Istrian coast the summer yield is outstanding, but the season is typically limited to 3–4 months, and tourist letting requires a categorisation decision (rješenje o kategorizaciji) and flat-rate taxation. On an annual basis, net yields typically fall between 3% and 5%.

AL.intro: Albania has the lowest entry threshold of any Mediterranean coastal market: on the Albanian Riviera (Sarandë, Ksamil, Himarë) the per-square-metre price of new-build sea-view apartments is still a fraction of Croatian or Greek levels, while Corfu and the Greek border are a stone's throw away. There is effectively no property transfer tax, and foreign buyers may purchase apartments in their own name. Tourism has grown at record rates in recent years, lifting short-term letting yields into the top tier of the region — in exchange, the market is younger, and legal due diligence (title, building permit) matters here more than anywhere.
highlights: The lowest coastal per-square-metre prices in the region
The BUYER pays no ad valorem transfer tax — only a fixed notary charge and registration fee
No VAT on the buyer's side for new-build either: property sales are VAT-exempt
Foreign individuals may buy apartments and houses in their own name, without consent
To buy a building PLOT, the value of the development must exceed three times the value of the land
Foreigners may not buy agricultural land, only lease it

faq.q: Is it safe to buy property in Albania as a foreigner?
faq.a: Legally, yes — a foreign individual may buy an apartment in their own name, and ownership can be registered with the cadastre (ASHK). The risk lies not in acquiring title but in the history behind it: the Albanian market does contain properties with unresolved title or built without permits, so never pay a deposit here without due diligence carried out by an independent lawyer.

faq.q: How much does a coastal apartment in Albania cost?
faq.a: In Ksamil and Sarandë, new-build sea-view apartments typically range from €1,200 to €2,200 per m², and entry prices in Vlorë and Durrës are lower still. That is roughly a half to a third of comparable Croatian or Greek stock. Buyer's transaction costs are also the lowest in the region: with no ad valorem transfer tax and no VAT, they typically come to 1–4% of the purchase price.

faq.q: Does Albania grant residence in exchange for buying property?
faq.a: Albania has neither a Golden Visa programme nor a citizenship-by-investment programme: the option set out in the 2020 legislation was never launched, has been under a moratorium since March 2023, and following the Court of Justice of the EU's April 2025 ruling on Malta, Albania — a country preparing for EU accession — is unlikely to open it. Property ownership does, however, support an application for a renewable one-year residence permit, leading to permanent residence after five years. The '€100,000 threshold' cited by agents cannot be traced to any legislation — do not plan around it.

RS.intro: Serbia is primarily an urban rental market rather than a coastal holiday market. The residential markets of Belgrade (particularly Belgrade Waterfront, Vračar and Novi Beograd) and Novi Sad are kept tight by strong internal migration, a growing IT sector and large numbers of arriving foreign workers — which means stable, year-round long-term rental demand, in contrast to the seasonality of Adriatic markets. The mountain resorts (Zlatibor, Kopaonik) are the terrain for two-season apartment investment. Transfer tax is 2.5%, replaced by VAT on new-build.
highlights: Year-round, non-seasonal rental demand in Belgrade and Novi Sad
Property transfer tax of 2.5% on second-hand property (by law payable by the SELLER, though contracts typically pass it to the buyer)
On new-build, 10% VAT (residential property) is included in the price instead
Foreign buyers may purchase on the basis of RECIPROCITY — this is satisfied for Hungarian citizens, but must be checked country by country
First-home reliefs are available only to Serbian citizens — foreign buyers cannot claim them
A two-season mountain apartment market (Zlatibor, Kopaonik)

faq.q: Can a Hungarian citizen buy property in Serbia?
faq.a: Yes. Serbia permits property acquisition by foreign individuals on the basis of reciprocity, and reciprocity with Hungary is in place (Hungary appears on the ministry's list), so a Hungarian citizen may buy an apartment or house in their own name. Importantly, reciprocity is assessed COUNTRY BY COUNTRY, not at EU level — so it is not true that 'any EU citizen may buy freely'. EU individuals are in principle entitled to buy agricultural land, but subject to cumulative conditions (10 years of local residence, 3 years of cultivation, a 2-hectare cap) that are all but impossible to meet in practice.

faq.q: What rental yield can be achieved in Belgrade?
faq.a: Gross yields on long-term residential letting in Belgrade typically run between 4% and 6%, higher than in most EU capitals, and demand is year-round — it does not depend on the tourist season.

faq.q: What does buying property in Serbia cost?
faq.a: On second-hand property, transfer tax is 2.5% (porez na prenos apsolutnih prava). The law imposes it on the SELLER, but contracts almost always shift it to the buyer — the seller nonetheless remains liable, so it is worth verifying that the payment has been made. On the first sale of a new-build completed after 2005, VAT applies in place of transfer tax: 10% on residential property, 20% on garages and commercial premises. Notary fees follow a banded tariff (a few hundred euros on an average apartment — the '1–2% notary fee' figure is wrong), plus legal fees and 2–4% agency commission. Only Serbian citizens qualify for the first-home tax exemption and VAT refund.

TR.intro: Turkey operates the world's highest-volume property-based citizenship programme: by buying property worth USD 400,000 and holding it for three years, the investor and their family can obtain Turkish CITIZENSHIP — not merely a residence permit — typically within 3 to 6 months and with no residence requirement. The Turkish passport gives visa-free entry to roughly 118 countries and opens the route to the US E-2 investor visa. The market rests on two legs: Istanbul (urban letting and capital growth) and the Mediterranean coast (Antalya, Alanya, Bodrum, Fethiye) for holiday and rental use. Transfer tax is 4%, and fixing the purchase price in foreign currency is mandatory under the programme.
highlights: USD 400,000 property purchase → Turkish CITIZENSHIP within 3–6 months
No residence, language or settlement requirement
The whole family (spouse + children under 18) can be included
The property must be held for 3 years, after which it can be sold freely
Visa-free entry to roughly 118 countries; a route to the US E-2 investor visa

faq.q: How can Turkish citizenship be obtained by buying property?
faq.a: You must buy Turkish property worth at least USD 400,000 and undertake not to sell it for three years — this is recorded on the title deed (tapu) as a 'satılamaz' (not for sale) annotation. Importantly, what counts is not the contract price but the official valuation by an appraiser licensed by the SPK; the threshold must be met by the price actually paid (evidenced through a bank), by the valuation, and by the value stated on the tapu alike. The residence permit and citizenship applications can then be submitted; the full process typically takes 3–6 months and extends to the spouse and children under 18.

faq.q: Do you have to live in Turkey to obtain citizenship?
faq.a: No. The Turkish citizenship-by-investment programme has no residence, language or examination requirement — you do not need to move to the country, and most of the application can in fact be handled by an authorised lawyer.

faq.q: Can I sell the property after obtaining citizenship?
faq.a: Yes, but only once the mandatory three-year holding period has elapsed. Citizenship is retained thereafter — Turkish citizenship is not revoked because the property has been sold.

faq.q: What are the transaction costs of buying property in Turkey?
faq.a: 4% tapu (transfer) duty, roughly 0.5% in notary and translation fees, legal fees of around 1.2%, and typically 2% agency commission. Under the citizenship programme you must add the mandatory valuation and the official processing fees.

ID.intro: Bali is a strong short-term letting market, but the real numbers are far more modest than advertised yields suggest: independent booking data put the average daily rate in Canggu at around USD 216, while occupancy is only about 38% and supply is expanding by 40% a year. Realistically, you should budget for 6–9% gross and 3–5% net, not the 10–15% quoted in brochures. The other key question is the form of ownership: a foreign individual CANNOT acquire freehold (Hak Milik) title in Indonesia. There are three legal routes: a contractual leasehold right (Hak Sewa, typically 25–30 years), a Hak Pakai right of use tied to a residence permit (30 + 20 + 30 = up to 80 years), or a Hak Guna Bangunan right to build held through an Indonesian PT PMA company (likewise 80 years). In Bali, choosing the legal structure matters more than choosing the property.
highlights: Realistic yields based on independent data: 6–9% gross, 3–5% net (10–15% is a marketing figure)
Foreigners CANNOT acquire freehold (Hak Milik) title — this is a constitutional restriction
Hak Sewa leasehold right: available without a residence permit, typically 25–30 years
Hak Pakai and PT PMA-held Hak Guna Bangunan: 30 + 20 + 30, up to 80 years in total
DANGER: the 'nominee' structure (freehold registered in an Indonesian proxy's name) is NULL AND VOID UNDER THE LAW
If a Hak Pakai owner's residence permit expires, they must sell the property within one year
Buyer's transaction costs: 2–4% on a leasehold right, 7–9% on Hak Pakai (because of 5% BPHTB)

faq.q: Does Indonesia grant residence in exchange for buying property?
faq.a: Only in a limited way. The property-based branch of the Indonesian Golden Visa requires an investment of USD 1,000,000 and applies EXCLUSIVELY to strata-title apartments with appropriate title — villas on individual plots do not qualify. There is also a 'second home' visa, but the official site publishes no specific value threshold, so the figures quoted by agents should be treated with caution. Indonesia has no citizenship-by-investment programme.

faq.q: Can a foreigner buy property in Bali?
faq.a: A foreign individual cannot acquire full freehold (Hak Milik) title in Indonesia — the 1960 basic law reserves it for Indonesian citizens. There are three legal solutions: a contractual leasehold right (Hak Sewa, typically 25–30 years, with no residence permit required), a Hak Pakai right of use (tied to a valid KITAS/KITAP residence permit, 30 + 20 + 30 = up to 80 years), and a Hak Guna Bangunan right to build held by a foreign-owned Indonesian company (PT PMA, likewise 80 years). VERY IMPORTANT: the 'nominee' structure frequently sold in Bali, under which an Indonesian citizen holds the freehold 'for your benefit', is NULL AND VOID under the law — it is unenforceable in court, and the property remains with the proxy or with the state.

faq.q: What yield does a Bali villa produce?
faq.a: Less than advertised. According to independent booking data, in Canggu — where close to 4,000 active listings compete — the average daily rate is around USD 216 and annual occupancy is about 38%, which works out at average revenue of roughly USD 23,700 a year. From that you must deduct villa management (15–20%), maintenance and tax. Realistically, plan for 6–9% gross and 3–5% net. Promises of 10–15% net come from sales materials and are not borne out by market data — supply is growing at over 40% a year while demand is flat, so owners are conceding 10–30% off asking prices.

faq.q: What happens when the leasehold expires?
faq.a: A well-drafted lease contains an extension option at a pre-agreed or index-linked price. That is precisely the most important check before buying: how many years remain, what the extension right is worth, and how the landowner's consent is secured. Without this, the value of the investment declines year after year. Hak Pakai carries a further trap: if the owner's residence permit expires and is not renewed, they are obliged to sell the property within one year, failing which it may be put up for state auction.

HU.intro: Hungary relaunched its investor residence programme in 2024, but one important misconception needs clearing up: under the Guest Investor Programme, BUYING A HOME DIRECTLY DOES NOT QUALIFY for a residence permit. The planned €500,000 direct property option was deleted in January 2025 before it ever took effect. Two routes remain: €250,000 of units in a real estate fund registered with the Hungarian National Bank (MNB) whose net asset value is at least 40% invested in Hungarian residential property, with a five-year holding requirement; or a €1,000,000 non-refundable donation to a Hungarian higher education institution. The Hungarian property market has two engines, Budapest and Lake Balaton; the transfer duty on property acquired for consideration is 4% up to one billion forints.
highlights: ATTENTION: buying a home directly DOES NOT QUALIFY for a guest investor residence permit
Only real estate fund units (€250,000, 5-year holding) or a higher education donation (€1,000,000) count under the programme
The permit runs for 10 years and can be extended once by a further 10 years
Transfer duty on property acquired for consideration is 4% up to one billion forints and 2% above that (capped at HUF 200 million per property)
5% VAT on new homes (apartments up to 150 m², houses up to 300 m²) until the end of 2026
Budapest: year-round yields underpinned by student and tenant demand
Lake Balaton: a domestic holiday market in sustained appreciation

faq.q: Can I obtain Hungarian residence if I buy an apartment in Hungary?
faq.a: No. This is the most common misconception about the Hungarian programme. Under the Guest Investor Programme, buying an apartment or house directly does NOT count as an investment: the planned €500,000 direct property option was deleted in January 2025 before it took effect. There are two options: buying €250,000 of units in a real estate fund registered with the MNB that invests at least 40% in Hungarian residential property (with a 5-year holding requirement), or a €1,000,000 donation to a Hungarian higher education institution. The permit runs for 10 years and can be extended once by a further 10 years. Hungarian and EU citizens of course have no need for it — they enjoy freedom of movement.

faq.q: How much duty is payable on a Hungarian property purchase?
faq.a: The transfer duty on property acquired for consideration is 4% on the portion of market value up to one billion forints and 2% on the portion above, capped at HUF 200 million per property. On a new home bought from a developer, the duty is waived up to a value of HUF 15 million, with 4% payable on the excess above that. Reliefs or exemptions may be claimed by young first-time buyers, on purchases using CSOK, and where the sale and purchase take place within five years of each other — in which case duty is payable only on the price difference. VAT on new homes is 5% rather than 27%, for apartments up to 150 m² and houses up to 300 m², until the end of 2026.

faq.q: What rental yield is achievable in Budapest?
faq.a: Gross yields on long-term residential letting in Budapest are typically 4–6%, and can be higher on small flats in the centre near the universities. Short-term letting yields more, but is subject to rules that differ from district to district.

TH.intro: Of the South-East Asian markets, Thailand offers foreigners the most predictable ownership structure: a foreign individual may acquire a condominium unit IN THEIR OWN NAME, as freehold — provided that no more than 49% of the total area of the condominium in question is in foreign hands. Foreigners may not, however, own land or a house together with its land; there, a long-term lease or a corporate structure is customary. Bangkok is the urban letting market, Phuket and Koh Samui the tourist-yield markets, and Chiang Mai the market for long-staying digital nomads.
highlights: Condominium units can be acquired freehold, in your own name, as a foreigner
A 49% foreign quota per condominium, measured by FLOOR AREA — to be verified in writing before buying
The purchase price must be remitted from abroad in foreign currency (no transfer without an FET certificate)
Foreigners may not own land; the statutory maximum for a registrable lease is 30 years
The '30+30+30' extension is only a contractual promise — the courts have not enforced it against a new owner
Transfer fee of 2%, customarily split between the parties — the buyer's total cost is typically 1–2%
ATTENTION: the 0.01% reduced fee applies ONLY to Thai citizens, not to foreign buyers

faq.q: Can a foreigner buy property in Thailand?
faq.a: A condominium unit, yes — in your own name, as freehold — provided that no more than 49% of the building's registered saleable floor area is in foreign ownership. The purchase price must be remitted from abroad in foreign currency, and the Land Department will not register title without the FET certificate evidencing this. A foreign individual may not own land, or a house built on land; in such cases a lease registrable for a maximum of 30 years, a usufruct, or a Thai-majority company are the available options. Caution: a Thai-majority company is lawful only with genuine Thai owners — a nominee structure breaches the Foreign Business Act, and the authorities actively investigate it.

faq.q: Is it true that Thailand is raising the foreign quota to 75% and leases to 99 years?
faq.a: No, this is NOT the law at present. In 2024 the government approved only that the two proposals be examined; no legislation ever came of them. The 99-year lease plan was formally removed from the agenda in September 2025. As of mid-2026, the rule in force remains a 49% foreign quota and a 30-year maximum registrable lease. Anyone buying a property priced on a 99-year horizon is paying for a right that does not exist.

faq.q: What does the 49% foreign quota mean?
faq.a: In every Thai condominium, no more than 49% of the registered saleable floor area may be in foreign ownership — the limit is therefore proportional to floor area, not to the number of units. Before buying, you must obtain written confirmation from the condominium's management that there is still room within the quota; without it the unit cannot be transferred into a foreign buyer's name, and the deposit can easily become stuck.

faq.q: What are the transaction costs of buying in Thailand?
faq.a: A 2% transfer fee on the official assessed value, customarily split between the parties. The seller bears the specific business tax (3.3% where the property has been held for less than five years) or stamp duty instead (0.5% where held for at least five years), plus withholding tax. The parties' combined costs typically come to 6–8% of the purchase price, but under the customary split the FOREIGN BUYER's share is only about 1–2%, including legal and due diligence fees. Importantly, the much-cited 0.01% reduced transfer fee applies exclusively to Thai citizens — foreign buyers and Thai companies pay the full 2%.

IT.intro: Italy is the EU's deepest and most differentiated holiday and urban property market: from the premium segment of Lake Como and Tuscany, through Milan's rental market, to the '€1 house' schemes of the south, every price level is available. EU citizens may buy without restriction. Taxation is the key: transfer tax on a second-hand property bought from a private individual is 9% (for a second home), but can fall to 2% for a buyer who becomes resident, and those relocating from abroad can access the €200,000 annual flat tax on foreign income.
highlights: EU member state — no purchase restrictions for EU citizens
Transfer tax of 9% on a second home, 2% if it becomes your 'prima casa' (main home); minimum €1,000
When buying from a private individual, the tax base is the cadastral value (prezzo-valore), typically well below the purchase price
VAT on a new property bought from a developer: 4% main home, 10% second home, 22% luxury category
There is NO property-based Golden Visa — property is expressly excluded from the Italian investor visa
Flat tax on foreign income for new residents: €300,000 a year from 1 January 2026

faq.q: How much tax does a buyer pay on an Italian property purchase?
faq.a: On a second-hand property bought from a private individual, transfer tax (imposta di registro) is 9% where it is a second home, and only 2% where the buyer registers it as their main home within 18 months; the minimum in both cases is €1,000. In sales between private individuals, the tax base may on request be the cadastral value (prezzo-valore), which is typically well below the actual purchase price — a meaningful saving. To this are added two fixed charges of €50 each. On a new property bought from a developer within 5 years of completion, VAT applies to the purchase: 4% for a main home, 10% for a second home, and 22% in the A/1, A/8 and A/9 luxury cadastral categories, plus three fixed charges of €200 each.

faq.q: Can a Hungarian citizen buy property in Italy?
faq.a: Yes, without restriction — both countries are EU member states, so a Hungarian buyer purchases on the same terms as Italians. An Italian tax number (codice fiscale) and an Italian bank account are required for the purchase.

faq.q: What is the Italian flat tax for new residents?
faq.a: Anyone transferring their tax residence to Italy may elect a regime under which a fixed annual amount of tax is paid on their foreign-source income, for up to 15 years. The 2026 budget law raised the amount to €300,000 a year for those transferring their residence from 1 January 2026 (with a further €50,000 per family member). Those who entered earlier keep the old figure: €100,000 for entrants before 2024 and €200,000 for entrants in 2024–2025. Importantly, this is a tax construct, NOT a residence programme — buying Italian property does not in itself confer a right of residence.

GR.intro: Greece operates the EU's best-known Golden Visa programme: a property investment secures a five-year, renewable residence permit that covers the whole family and grants free movement within Schengen, WITH NO MINIMUM STAY REQUIREMENT — the programme's strongest argument. Since Law 5100/2024 the threshold has been banded by area: €800,000 across the whole Attica region, in Thessaloniki, on Mykonos and Santorini and on islands with populations above 3,100; €400,000 in the rest of the country; and €250,000 EXCLUSIVELY for converting a non-residential building into a dwelling or restoring a listed building. Two conditions are frequently left unmentioned: the property must be a single unit with at least 120 m² of main living area, and short-term (Airbnb-style) letting of a Golden Visa property is PROHIBITED — breaching the rule means revocation of the permit and a €50,000 fine.
highlights: Golden Visa bands: €800,000 (Attica, Thessaloniki, Mykonos, Santorini, larger islands) / €400,000 (elsewhere) / €250,000 (conversion or listed-building restoration only)
The property must be a single unit, with a minimum of 120 m² of main living area
Short-term letting of the Golden Visa property is PROHIBITED — the penalty is revocation and a €50,000 fine
There is NO minimum stay requirement
It covers the whole family (spouse, children under 21, dependent parents of both spouses)
Valid for 5 years, renewable for as long as the investment is maintained
Property transfer tax of 3% (plus a small municipal surcharge)
The VAT suspension on new-build has been extended to 31 December 2026 — the 3% transfer tax applies in such cases too

faq.q: How does the Greek Golden Visa work?
faq.a: By buying Greek real estate you can apply for a five-year, renewable residence permit that extends to the spouse, children under 21 and the dependent parents of both parties. The permit grants Schengen free movement and does not require minimum residence — it is enough to maintain the investment.

faq.q: What is the threshold of the Greek Golden Visa?
faq.a: There are three bands under Law 5100/2024. 800 000 € in the entire region of Attica, in the regional unit of Thessaloniki, on Mykonos, Santorini and every island with a population above 3 100. 400 000 € across all other parts of the country. 250 000 € only in two cases: if a non-residential building is converted into a home (the works must be completed before the application), or a listed heritage building is renovated. The 2024 transitional deadlines have all expired, so in 2026 the full 400/800 thousand threshold applies. Before buying, always check the specific zoning classification of the given property.

faq.q: Can I let my Greek Golden Visa property short-term?
faq.a: No. The 2024 reform expressly prohibits the short-term (Airbnb-style) letting of the property underlying the Golden Visa. Breaching the ban leads to withdrawal of the residence permit and a 50 000 euro fine. Long-term letting is permitted. This condition radically changes the yield calculation of the investment, yet it is missing from most offers.

faq.q: Does the Greek Golden Visa grant citizenship?
faq.a: Not directly. The Golden Visa is a residence permit; Greek citizenship can be applied for after seven years of actual residence in Greece, subject to language and integration conditions. If the goal is the passport itself, through real estate investment it is Turkey that grants citizenship directly.

ES.intro: Spain is Europe's highest-turnover holiday property market: the stock on the Costa del Sol (Marbella, Málaga), the Costa Blanca (Alicante), the Balearics and Barcelona is the deepest and most easily resold on the continent. Because of the international buyer base, the exit (sale) is substantially quicker than in smaller markets. One important legislative change: the property-based route into the Spanish Golden Visa was abolished in April 2025, so buying Spanish property today does NOT confer an investor residence permit — the purchase is purely an investment and lifestyle decision. Transfer tax ranges from 6% to 10% depending on the region.
highlights: Europe's largest and most liquid holiday property market — quick resale
Property transfer tax (ITP) of roughly 6–11% depending on the region: Madrid 6%, Andalusia 7%, Catalonia 10–11%
ATTENTION: the property-based Golden Visa was abolished in April 2025
An extensive Hungarian and international community along the southern coast

faq.q: Does the Spanish Golden Visa through property purchase still exist?
faq.a: No. Spain abolished the real-estate-investment route of the Golden Visa in April 2025, so a Spanish investor residence permit can no longer be obtained by buying property worth 500 000 euros. Within the EU, Greece and Hungary offer a property-based settlement programme; citizenship through real estate is granted primarily by Turkey.

faq.q: How much tax does the buyer pay in Spain?
faq.a: On a resale property the buyer is charged roughly 6–11% transfer tax (ITP) depending on the region: 6% in Madrid, 7% flat in Andalusia, 10% in Catalonia up to one million euros and 11% above, 10% in Valencia (9% from 1 June 2026, 11% above one million euros). The tax base is the higher of the purchase price and the official cadastral reference value — this is important because even with a low contract price the tax is levied on the reference value. On a newly built property bought from a developer, 10% VAT plus a region-dependent 0.5–1.5% stamp duty (AJD) is payable.

faq.q: What do you need as a Hungarian buyer to purchase Spanish real estate?
faq.a: A Spanish tax identification number (NIE), a Spanish bank account and typically an independent lawyer. As an EU citizen no purchase restriction applies to you, and the process concludes before a notary (escritura pública).

AE.intro: Dubai is the highest-turnover, entirely tax-free property market on the Persian Gulf: in the designated freehold zones (Dubai Marina, Downtown, Palm Jumeirah, Business Bay) a foreign buyer of any nationality can acquire full ownership in their own name, without a residence permit. There is no annual property tax, no personal income tax, no capital gains tax, and rental income earned by a private individual is not taxed either. Gross rental yields average around 7% across the city — outstanding by international standards — and a property worth 2,000,000 AED (~505,000 €) qualifies the owner for a renewable 10-year Golden Visa. Two things matter: outside the designated zones a foreigner cannot buy, so before purchase the classification of the specific property must be checked with the Dubai Land Department; and the UAE does NOT grant citizenship for property — only residence.
highlights: A foreigner of any nationality can buy FREEHOLD property in their own name in the designated zones, without a residence permit
No annual property tax, income tax, capital gains tax or rental tax for the private individual owner
Property transfer fee of 4% (officially 2% buyer + 2% seller; in practice the buyer pays the full amount)
From 2,000,000 AED (~505,000 €) a renewable 10-year Golden Visa — extends to family members too
The Golden Visa is RESIDENCE, not citizenship — the UAE does not grant a passport for property
Gross rental yield of ~7% on the city average; the dirham is pegged to the US dollar
Outside the designated zones a foreigner cannot buy — the zone must be verified with the DLD

faq.q: Can a foreigner buy property in Dubai?
faq.a: Yes. A foreign private individual of any nationality can buy full-ownership (freehold) property in their own name, without a residence permit, in Dubai's designated zones — such as Dubai Marina, Downtown, Palm Jumeirah and Business Bay. Outside these zones, however, ownership is reserved for UAE and GCC nationals, so before purchase it is advisable to check the classification of the specific property with the Dubai Land Department (DLD).

faq.q: How much tax is payable on a Dubai property?
faq.a: On purchase there is a 4% property transfer fee (DLD transfer fee) — officially 2% for the buyer and 2% for the seller, but in practice the buyer pays the full 4%. On top of this there is an approximately 4,200 AED trustee fee and a 250 AED title deed fee. Ownership, however, is effectively tax-free: there is no annual property tax, no personal income tax, no capital gains tax, and residential rental income earned by a private individual is not taxed either.

faq.q: Does Dubai grant a residence permit for buying property?
faq.a: Yes. A property worth at least 2,000,000 AED (~505,000 €) qualifies the owner for a renewable 10-year Golden Visa, which extends to the spouse, children (regardless of age) and parents, and carries no minimum stay requirement. Mortgaged property and, under certain conditions, off-plan property are also accepted. Important: this is a residence permit, NOT citizenship — the UAE does not grant a passport for property investment.

faq.q: Can you obtain citizenship in Dubai through property?
faq.a: No. The United Arab Emirates has no property-based citizenship programme. A passport can only be obtained through official nomination (for prominent investors, doctors, scientists and talents), which cannot be purchased and cannot be applied for. Through property investment in Dubai the 10-year Golden Visa (residence) is the most that is available; in our portfolio, citizenship through property is offered by Turkey.

faq.q: What rental yield can be achieved in Dubai?
faq.a: The gross rental yield on Dubai apartments averages around 7% across the city (according to independent data in mid-2026, roughly 6.9–7.2%), which is outstanding by international standards. In the more affordable, up-and-coming zones (for example Jumeirah Village Circle, Dubai Sports City) 7.5–8.5% is achievable, while in premium locations (Downtown, Palm Jumeirah) it is 5–6%. From the net yield the service charge must be deducted, which can be significant depending on the location.

allampolgarsag-ingatlanbefektetessel.title: Citizenship through real estate investment: where can you truly obtain a passport?
allampolgarsag-ingatlanbefektetessel.description: In which country does buying property grant citizenship directly? The thresholds, deadlines and pitfalls of the Turkish programme, and why no Golden Visa grants EU citizenship.
allampolgarsag-ingatlanbefektetessel.answer: Direct citizenship — not just residence — through real estate investment can today be obtained primarily in Turkey: with property worth 400 000 USD, extending to the whole family, typically within 3–6 months and with no obligation to settle. A few Caribbean countries operate a similar programme. By contrast, every EU route — the Greek, the Hungarian or the former Spanish Golden Visa — grants only a residence permit, not citizenship.

sections.h: The key distinction: citizenship or merely residence?
p: The market uses the term "Golden Visa" for two legally entirely different things, and most misunderstandings stem from this. A residence programme (residence by investment) grants a settlement permit: you may live in and move freely within the given country, but your passport does not change. A citizenship programme (citizenship by investment, CBI), by contrast, grants genuine citizenship and a passport, typically within a few months and without settling.

Direct citizenship through real estate investment, with a large, liquid market and a fast procedure, is today offered by Turkey — which is why this is the citizenship-by-investment route in Proopify's portfolio. A few Caribbean countries also operate a property-based citizenship programme, but those are small, programme-driven markets with a slower exit. In the EU there is NO such programme: the Maltese citizenship programme had to be halted following the 2025 ruling of the European Court of Justice, while the Greek and Hungarian programmes are solely about residence permits.

sections.h: Turkey: citizenship from real estate within 3–6 months
p: The world's largest-volume property-based citizenship programme is Turkey's. The condition: the purchase of Turkish property worth at least 400 000 USD, which must be held for three years — this is entered on the title deed (tapu) as a "satılamaz" (not for sale) notation. After this the applicant receives a residence permit and then citizenship, typically within 3–6 months. The programme extends to the spouse and children under 18; adult children and parents cannot be included.

head: Aspect
Turkey 🇹🇷
rows: Eligibility for the US E-2 investor visa

sections.h: The pitfall the ads keep quiet about: the valuation
p: The most common mistake with the Turkish programme is that the buyer looks at the contract price. Yet that is not what counts, but the official valuation by an SPK-licensed appraiser — and the 400 000 USD threshold must be met SIMULTANEOUSLY by the price paid and verified through the bank, by the valuation and by the value shown on the title deed. A high contract price cannot "correct" a low valuation, and the threshold is measured in USD, so the exchange rate matters too.

The other common misunderstanding concerns the US E-2 visa. It is true that a Turkish citizen — unlike a Hungarian one — becomes eligible to apply for the E-2 investor visa. But the E-2 is not a settlement permit and not a green card; it is a renewable non-immigrant visa tied to an investment, and for naturalised citizens several years of citizenship are in practice expected for it.

sections.h: What to clarify before you decide, no matter what
p: 1. Whether the valuation meets the threshold — in Turkey it is not the contract price but the official SPK valuation that counts.
2. The tax consequences in your own jurisdiction: acquiring citizenship does not in itself terminate Hungarian tax residency.
3. The exit: at what real price the property can be sold on after the three-year holding period. This is the most frequently underestimated risk of CBI investments.
4. If the passport is not the main goal but EU settlement is: then you should look not for a CBI but for a Golden Visa — we have a separate article on that.

faq.q: In which country can you obtain citizenship by buying real estate?
faq.a: With a large, liquid property market and a fast procedure, primarily in Turkey: with property worth 400 000 USD, extending to the whole family, typically within 3–6 months and with no obligation to settle. A few Caribbean countries also operate a property-based citizenship programme, but those are small, less liquid markets. In an EU member state you cannot obtain citizenship directly through real estate.

faq.q: Can you obtain EU citizenship through real estate investment?
faq.a: No. At present, in no EU member state can you obtain citizenship directly by buying real estate. The Greek and Hungarian programmes grant a residence permit, the Spanish property-based Golden Visa ended in April 2025, and the Maltese citizenship programme had to be halted following the 2025 ruling of the European Court of Justice.

faq.q: Do you have to keep the property after obtaining citizenship?
faq.a: Yes, for a while. In Turkey the property may not be sold for three years (this is also registered on the title deed). Citizenship remains afterwards, even after selling the property — Turkish citizenship is not withdrawn because of a sale.

faq.q: Will I lose my Hungarian citizenship?
faq.a: No. Hungary accepts dual citizenship, and Turkey does not require renouncing one's previous citizenship either. The question of tax residency is independent of this, however — it is worth clarifying that with a tax adviser.

golden-visa-ingatlannal.title: Golden Visa through real estate investment in 2026: which country gives what?
golden-visa-ingatlannal.description: Greece, Hungary and the other programmes: thresholds, residence conditions, and which route leads to citizenship. A fresh, comparative overview.
golden-visa-ingatlannal.answer: Through DIRECT property purchase, in the EU today you can obtain a residence permit essentially only in Greece: from 400 000 € across most of the country, from 800 000 € in Attica, Thessaloniki and the popular islands. In the Hungarian guest investor programme direct home purchase does NOT qualify — there only an investment unit in a real estate fund (250 000 €) or a higher-education donation (1 000 000 €) counts. The Spanish property-based Golden Visa ended on 3 April 2025, and in Italy real estate is expressly excluded from the investor visa. If the goal is the passport itself, you need not a residence programme but a citizenship one — through real estate this is offered primarily by Turkey.

sections.h: What does a Golden Visa actually give?
p: The Golden Visa is a residence permit that the state grants in exchange for an investment — typically a property purchase. For an EU programme this also means Schengen free movement, that is, in most cases visa-free travel throughout the zone. What it does NOT give: a passport and citizenship. In most countries citizenship can only be applied for after several years of actual residence, a language exam and a naturalisation procedure.

As a Hungarian citizen you do not need a Golden Visa for free movement within the EU — which is why for domestic buyers these programmes are of interest primarily for tax-planning, business or "plan B" considerations, or in the case of third-country family members.

sections.h: The programmes side by side
p: The thresholds show each country's official, property-based entry level.

head: Country
Threshold
What it gives
Residence on site
rows: 800 000 € (Attica, Thessaloniki, large islands) / 400 000 € (elsewhere) / 250 000 € (conversion only, listed building)
5-year, renewable residence permit, for the whole family
250 000 € real estate FUND unit or 1 000 000 € donation
10-year permit — DIRECT home purchase does NOT qualify
ENDED: the property-based route closed on 3 April 2025
NONE: real estate is excluded from the investor visa

sections.h: Greece: the best-known EU programme
p: The Greek Golden Visa runs for five years, renewable as long as the investment is maintained, and extends to the spouse, children under 21 and the dependent parents of both parties. Its strongest feature is that there is no minimum residence requirement.

Since Law 5100/2024 the threshold has three bands. 800 000 € in the entire region of Attica, in the regional unit of Thessaloniki, on Mykonos, Santorini and every island with a population above 3 100. 400 000 € across all other parts of the country. 250 000 € only in two cases: converting a non-residential building into a home (the works must be completed BEFORE submitting the application), or renovating a listed heritage building. The 2024 transitional deadlines have all expired, so in 2026 the full threshold applies.

Two conditions that are missing from most offers, yet may decide the investment: in the 400 and 800 thousand euro band the property must be a SINGLE unit, with at least 120 m² of main living area (the investment cannot be split across several dwellings), and the property underlying the Golden Visa MAY NOT be let short-term, Airbnb-style. Breaching the ban leads to withdrawal of the permit and a 50 000 euro fine. For anyone who planned the Greek programme around tourist rental yield, this fundamentally rewrites the calculation.

sections.h: Hungary: the guest investor programme relaunched in 2024
p: Here the most important thing is to clear up a widespread misunderstanding: in the Hungarian Guest Investor Programme DIRECT HOME PURCHASE does NOT QUALIFY for a residence permit. The direct property option of 500 000 euros originally planned in the legislation was deleted in January 2025, before it even took effect. So anyone who simply buys a Budapest flat will not receive a guest investor permit.

Two routes remain. One is buying a 250 000 € investment unit in a real estate fund registered with the MNB, whose net asset value is at least 40% in Hungarian residential property, with a five-year holding obligation. The other is a 1 000 000 € non-refundable donation to a Hungarian higher-education institution. The permit runs for ten years and can be extended once by a further ten years — the longest term in the EU — and the investment must be made within 93 days of first entry on the guest investor visa.
For Hungarian and EU citizens this programme is naturally not relevant, since they enjoy free movement; its target audience is the third-country investor.

sections.h: If the goal is the passport, do not look for a Golden Visa
p: The most common mistake is that someone wants citizenship and buys a Golden Visa for it. The two are not the same and do not lead automatically into one another: Greek naturalisation, for example, requires seven years of actual residence in Greece, a language exam and an integration exam — the Golden Visa does not substitute for this on its own.

If the real goal is a second passport, then you should choose not a residence but a citizenship programme — through real estate this is offered primarily by Turkey. We wrote about this in detail in our article "Citizenship through real estate investment".

faq.q: In which country can you get a Golden Visa today by buying real estate?
faq.a: Through direct property purchase essentially only in Greece: from 400 000 euros across most of the country, from 800 000 euros in Attica, Thessaloniki and the popular islands, and from 250 000 euros only for building conversion or heritage renovation. In the Hungarian programme direct home purchase does not count — there you need a real estate fund investment unit (250 000 €) or a higher-education donation (1 000 000 €). The Spanish property-based Golden Visa ended on 3 April 2025, and in Italy real estate is excluded from the investor visa.

faq.q: Does the Golden Visa grant EU citizenship?
faq.a: No. The Golden Visa is a residence permit. Citizenship can only be obtained through the ordinary naturalisation procedure, which typically requires several years of actual residence, a language exam and an integration exam — in the Greek case, for example, seven years.

faq.q: Do you have to live in the country to keep the Golden Visa?
faq.a: In the Greek and Hungarian programmes there is no minimum residence requirement: it is enough to maintain the investment and renew the permit. At the same time, this also means that these years do not count towards the residence period required for naturalisation — Greek naturalisation, for example, requires seven years of ACTUAL residence in Greece.

kulfoldi-ingatlan-mellekkoltsegek.title: What does it really cost? Ancillary costs of buying property abroad in 12 countries
kulfoldi-ingatlan-mellekkoltsegek.description: Transfer tax, notary, lawyer, agent commission — broken down by country. The total cost on top of the purchase price typically moves between 3–14%.
kulfoldi-ingatlan-mellekkoltsegek.answer: The ancillary cost on top of the purchase price when buying property abroad differs significantly by country: in Albania there is practically no transfer tax, in Montenegro 3%, in Hungary and Turkey 4%, while in Italy 9% (second home) and in Spain 6–10%. On top of this everywhere come the notary fee, the lawyer fee and typically a 2–5% agent commission.

sections.h: Why this matters more than the purchase price
p: When buying property abroad, most of the unpleasant surprises are not in the price but in the ancillary cost. On top of the purchase price, typically another 5–14% comes, depending on the country — for a 300,000-euro property that is a difference of between 15,000 and 42,000 euros purely depending on which country you buy in.

The table below shows the most important items by country. Important: the transfer tax typically applies to USED property — for a new build, in many countries VAT takes its place, which can be higher.

sections.h: The cheapest and the most expensive entry
p: The most favourable transaction cost is offered by Albania, where the buyer pays no value-based transfer tax at all, followed by Thailand (2%) and Serbia (2.5%). In Croatia the duty is a fixed 3%. Montenegro has applied a TIERED system since 2024: up to 150,000 € it is 3%, above that 5%, and above 500,000 € it is 6% — so on a 600,000-euro property the tax is not 18,000 but 28,000 €. At the other end of the scale stands Italy at 9% for a second home, as well as Spain, where the regional ITP moves between roughly 6–11%.

A common misconception is that the agent commission is always paid by the seller. This differs by market: in the Balkan and Turkish markets, splitting the commission or the buyer bearing it is also common, so this must be clarified in writing before the deposit.

sections.h: What to budget for beyond the table
p: Translation and certification: in many countries contracts must be submitted with a certified translation.
Tax identifier and bank account: in Spain a NIE, in Italy a codice fiscale, in Greece an AFM is required — without these you cannot contract.
Annual upkeep: property tax, common cost, insurance, and for a holiday property the winterisation or the management fee.
Currency conversion: for non-eurozone countries (Turkey, Thailand, Indonesia, Hungary) the bank conversion margin alone can be 1–3% — it is worth using a separate currency provider.

faq.q: What is the total ancillary cost of buying property abroad?
faq.a: Depending on the country, typically 5–14% of the purchase price. The lowest is in Albania and Thailand (about 3–6%), medium in Montenegro, Croatia, Hungary and Turkey (about 5–9%), and the highest in Italy and Spain (about 10–14%).

faq.q: Who pays the agent commission when buying property abroad?
faq.a: It differs by market. In Western European markets typically the seller, but in the Balkan and Turkish markets splitting or the buyer bearing it is also common. It must always be clarified in writing BEFORE paying the deposit.

faq.q: Do you also have to pay transfer tax on a new build?
faq.a: Usually not — in most countries the price of a new property bought from the developer includes VAT, and in that case there is no separate transfer tax. This is not necessarily cheaper, however: the Spanish 10% or the Italian 10–22% VAT can be higher than the transfer tax on a used property.

kulfoldi-ingatlanvasarlas-lepesei.title: Buying property abroad step by step: the complete process
kulfoldi-ingatlanvasarlas-lepesei.description: From the search to registration of title: due diligence, deposit, preliminary contract, notary, tax number and the most common mistakes that cost money.
kulfoldi-ingatlanvasarlas-lepesei.answer: Buying property abroad consists of six steps: (1) searching and shortlisting, (2) obtaining a local tax number and opening a bank account, (3) legal due diligence on the title carried out by an independent lawyer, (4) the preliminary contract and deposit (typically 10%), (5) the final contract before a notary and payment of the purchase price, (6) registration of title at the cadastre. The whole process typically takes 4–12 weeks.

sections.h: 1. Searching and shortlisting
p: The most common mistake is for the buyer to fall in love with the property rather than the market. First you must decide on the objective: rental yield, personal use, or a residence/citizenship programme. These three objectives often point to entirely different countries and different property types.

For yield, urban year-round rental demand (Belgrade, Budapest, Bangkok) is more predictable than the seasonal coast. For personal use, flight connections and winter occupancy are decisive. For programme purposes, the property's eligibility matters more than its location.

sections.h: 2. Local tax number and bank account
p: In almost every country a local tax number is required for the contract: NIE in Spain, codice fiscale in Italy, AFM in Greece, vergi numarası in Turkey. Obtaining these takes 1–3 weeks and can in many cases be handled remotely under a power of attorney.

A local bank account is needed not only for the purchase price but also for paying utilities and taxes afterwards. Be prepared for anti-money-laundering checks: the source of the purchase price must be evidenced with documents.

sections.h: 3. Legal due diligence — the most expensive place to economise
p: Engage an independent lawyer, one NOT recommended by the seller or the agent. The minimum scope of due diligence: the title register (who the registered owner is), encumbrances and mortgages, building and occupancy permits, the zoning classification of the land, service charge and tax arrears, and whether the floor area of the property matches what was advertised.

For an off-plan property bought from a developer, the bank guarantee or escrow structure is a separate question: what happens to the money you have paid if the developer fails to complete the building.

sections.h: 4. Preliminary contract and deposit
p: Established practice is a 10% deposit paid at the same time as the preliminary contract, which is typically returned twofold if the seller breaches the contract but forfeited if the buyer withdraws. Pay the deposit into a lawyer's escrow account where possible, rather than directly to the seller.
The preliminary contract must set out the deadline for the final contract, the precise condition of the property and its fixtures, and who bears each of the transaction costs.

sections.h: 5. Final contract and payment
p: In most European markets the final sale takes place before a notary (escritura, atto notarile, ugovor), who verifies the identity of the parties and the title. In Turkey, transfer of ownership takes place at the land registry (tapu).

Pay the purchase price by bank transfer and in a documented manner where possible — cash payment is restricted or prohibited in several countries, and makes it harder to evidence your acquisition cost on a later sale.

sections.h: 6. Registration and follow-up
p: Ownership becomes complete only when the cadastre registers it. This takes anywhere from a few days to a few months depending on the country. Next come transferring the utilities, registering for local property tax, and — if you intend to let the property — arranging the letting licence or categorisation.

Finally: settle your Hungarian tax position as well. Rental income from a foreign property and the capital gain on a later sale are taxed in accordance with the double taxation treaties — this varies by country, and paying the foreign tax does not always close the matter.

faq.q: How long does buying property abroad take?
faq.a: Typically 4–12 weeks from accepted offer to registration of title, on a cash purchase. Obtaining the local tax number takes 1–3 weeks, legal due diligence 1–2 weeks, and the notarial completion depends on the parties' timing.

faq.q: Do I need to engage a lawyer when buying property abroad?
faq.a: Yes, and preferably one who was not recommended by the seller or the agent. In most countries the notary checks the legality of the contract but does not represent your interests — checking encumbrances, permits and floor area is the lawyer's job.

faq.q: Can you buy remotely, without attending in person?
faq.a: In most countries yes, using a certified and apostilled power of attorney granted to a lawyer. In practice it is nonetheless worth viewing the property in person at least once — photographs are systematically more flattering than reality.

berleti-hozam-rangsor.title: Rental yield ranking: where is it highest across 12 countries?
berleti-hozam-rangsor.description: In which country is buying property to rent out the most worthwhile? The gross rental yields of 12 markets in one table, flagging seasonality and risks.
berleti-hozam-rangsor.answer: The highest gross rental yields are offered by Bali (6–9%), Dubai (~7%) and the emerging Balkan-Turkish urban markets (5–8%). The mature Western European markets (Italy, Spain) yield lower, 3–5%, but are more stable and liquid in return. The gross figure is misleading, however: the real NET yield, reduced by management and tax costs, is typically 2–4 percentage points lower.

sections.h: Gross rental yield by country
p: Gross rental yield is the annual rental income divided by the purchase price. This is the most commonly advertised figure, but the real return is shown by the net yield (after deducting management, maintenance, tax and vacancy). The table shows indicative average urban gross yields — it must always be recalculated for the specific property.

sections.h: Why is net yield more important than gross?
p: For tourism (short-term) rentals, the difference between gross and net yield is enormous. Professional villa management takes 15–20%, on top of which come maintenance, cleaning, the booking platform fee (Airbnb/Booking 3–15%) and vacancy. In Bali, a gross 6–9% realistically drops to a net 3–5%.
Long-term urban rental (Belgrade, Budapest) is more predictable: lower gross, but less turnover and no seasonality risk. For retirement or passive-income goals this is often the better choice.

Currency risk also matters: yields outside the eurozone (Turkish lira, Thai baht, Indonesian rupiah) may be high in local currency, but on conversion to forint/euro a currency depreciation can wipe out the profit.

faq.q: Which country has the highest rental yield?
faq.a: On a gross basis Bali (6–9%), Dubai (~7%) and the Albanian coast (6–8%) lead. On a net basis, however, urban year-round markets (Belgrade, Budapest, 4–6% gross) often perform similarly well, because they have no seasonality risk and lower management costs.

faq.q: What is the difference between gross and net yield?
faq.a: For tourism rentals, typically 2–4 percentage points. A Bali villa with an 8% gross yield often nets 4–5%. The difference comes from villa management (15–20%), platform fees, maintenance, tax and vacancy.

faq.q: Does currency risk matter for renting out?
faq.a: Yes, significantly. In countries outside the eurozone (Turkey, Thailand, Indonesia, Hungary) the yield arises in the local currency, and on conversion to forint or euro the exchange-rate movement can take away or add several percent per year. In Dubai the dirham is pegged to the dollar, which is more stable but means USD exposure.

legolcsobb-tengerparti-ingatlan-europa.title: The cheapest seaside property in Europe in 2026
legolcsobb-tengerparti-ingatlan-europa.description: Where can you buy a sea-view apartment most cheaply in Europe? The prices of Albania, Montenegro and the Balkans compared with the Croatian and Greek markets.
legolcsobb-tengerparti-ingatlan-europa.answer: The cheapest European seaside entry point is offered by Albania: on the Albanian Riviera (Ksamil, Sarandë) a newly built, sea-view apartment costs 1,200–2,200 €/m². This is followed by Montenegro's southern coast (Bar, Ulcinj) and the rest of the Balkans. Croatia and Greece are significantly more expensive (typically 2,500–5,000+ €/m² in good coastal locations), but in return you get EU membership and a more liquid market.

sections.h: Seaside entry prices compared
p: The per-square-metre price varies strongly by distance from the sea, the view and the year of construction. The ranges below apply to newly built or well-kept apartments close to the sea (not necessarily front-row), and are indicative only.

sections.h: The price of a cheap price: what to watch for
p: Albania has the lowest entry point, but the market is the youngest: properties with unclear title backgrounds or built without permits do occur. Without due diligence carried out by an independent lawyer (title deed in the ASHK cadastre, building permit), never pay a deposit here under any circumstances.

Montenegro is a good middle ground: you pay in euros, the transfer duty is among the lowest, and the foreign buyer purchases with practically the same rights as locals. In return, it is not an EU member (though a candidate).
Croatia and Greece are more expensive, but you get EU membership, Schengen free movement and significantly more liquid resale — if easy exit matters, this can outweigh the price advantage.

faq.q: Where is the cheapest seaside property in Europe?
faq.a: In Albania, on the Albanian Riviera (Ksamil, Sarandë): newly built, sea-view apartments move in the 1,200–2,200 €/m² range, which is a half to a third of comparable Croatian or Greek offerings. The buyer's ancillary cost is also the lowest, because in Albania there is no value-based transfer tax on the buyer's side.

faq.q: Is it safe to buy cheap seaside property in Albania?
faq.a: Legally yes — a foreigner can buy an apartment in their own name — but the main risk is the title background: properties built without a permit or with unclear title do occur. Due diligence carried out by an independent lawyer before the deposit is mandatory.

faq.q: Is it worth buying more expensively in an EU member state?
faq.a: If Schengen free movement, legal predictability and fast resale matter, then yes — Croatia and Greece are more liquid and more predictable. If the goal is maximum yield or the lowest entry point, Albania and Montenegro are better.

kulfoldi-ingatlan-adozasa-magyar-tulajdo.title: Taxation of foreign property as a Hungarian owner
kulfoldi-ingatlan-adozasa-magyar-tulajdo.description: How is the rental income and sale profit of a foreign property taxed for an owner with Hungarian tax residency? The logic of double-taxation treaties explained clearly.
kulfoldi-ingatlan-adozasa-magyar-tulajdo.answer: For a private individual with Hungarian tax residency, income from a foreign property can arise twice: in the country where the property is located AND in Hungary. Double taxation is resolved by the treaties concluded by Hungary — for real estate, the country where the property is LOCATED almost always taxes first, and Hungary either exempts the income or credits the tax paid abroad. The specific rule differs by country, so it is worth clarifying with a tax expert before investing.

sections.h: The key concept: tax residency
p: A Hungarian citizen typically remains a Hungarian tax resident even if they own property abroad — unless they move out and relocate the centre of their life. A Hungarian-resident private individual is taxed in Hungary on their WORLDWIDE income, so the income from the foreign property also falls within the scope of Hungarian taxation. Double tax is excluded by the treaties.

Important: the fact that you acquire citizenship or a Golden Visa abroad does NOT in itself terminate Hungarian tax residency. Residency is tied to actual presence and the centre of interests, not to the passport.

sections.h: Rental income
p: Under almost every treaty, the rental income of a foreign property is taxed first by the country where the property is located. Hungary can then proceed in two ways depending on the treaty: under the EXEMPTION method, rental income taxed abroad is not taxed again at home (though it may be taken into account with progression reservation), while under the CREDIT method it also calculates the tax at home but deducts the amount paid abroad.

There are places where the country of location does not, or barely, taxes a private individual's rental income (for example, in the United Arab Emirates there is no personal income tax) — in such cases it is especially important to check whether the Hungarian side exempts or credits, because the two give very different results.

sections.h: Sale (capital) gain
p: The gain from the sale of property is also, under the treaties, typically allowed to be taxed by the country of location. In Hungary, income from the sale of property can be tax-exempt after 5 years (from the fifth year following the year of acquisition), but for a foreign property one must always look at the given treaty and the country-of-location rule together.

For foreign-currency purchase and sale, the exchange rate also matters: the Hungarian tax base must be determined in forint, so the difference between the exchange rate at purchase and at sale in itself affects the calculated gain.

faq.q: Do I have to pay tax in Hungary on my foreign property?
faq.a: As a Hungarian tax resident you are also taxable at home on your worldwide income, but the double-taxation treaty excludes the same income being taxed twice. For real estate the country of location typically taxes first, and Hungary either exempts the income or credits the tax paid abroad. The specific method differs from treaty to treaty.

faq.q: Does citizenship or a Golden Visa terminate the Hungarian tax obligation?
faq.a: No. Tax residency is tied to actual presence and the centre of interests, not to the passport. If you do not move out and do not relocate the centre of your life, you remain a Hungarian resident, and your worldwide income is taxable at home as well.

faq.q: Where should I find out about the specific rule?
faq.a: From the double-taxation treaty between Hungary and the given country, and from a tax adviser specialising in international tax matters. This article is general information, not personalised tax advice — before a six-figure investment, always seek an expert opinion.

deviza-arfolyamkockazat-kulfoldi-ingatla.title: Currency and exchange-rate risk when buying property abroad
deviza-arfolyamkockazat-kulfoldi-ingatla.description: Euro, dollar, lira or dirham? In which currency does the risk arise across the 12 markets, and how to save on currency conversion when buying property abroad.
deviza-arfolyamkockazat-kulfoldi-ingatla.answer: Half of the 12 markets are eurozone or euro-priced (Montenegro, Croatia, Greece, Italy, Spain, Albania), so a Hungarian buyer only has EUR/HUF risk. In the rest, a separate currency comes into play: the Dubai dirham is pegged to the dollar (stable, but USD exposure), while the Turkish lira, the Thai baht and the Indonesian rupiah are volatile. The bank's currency-conversion margin alone can be 1–3% — much can be saved here with a dedicated currency provider.

sections.h: What is the currency exposure in each market?
p: Currency risk strikes at two points: when paying the purchase price (a one-off conversion) and when repatriating the rental income (a recurring conversion). In eurozone markets a Hungarian buyer has only one currency layer (EUR/HUF); in non-eurozone ones, two.

sections.h: How to reduce the conversion loss?
p: The currency-conversion margin of commercial banks (the difference between the buy and sell rate) is a serious sum on a larger property purchase: on 300,000 euros a 2% margin is 6,000 euros. With a dedicated currency provider (e.g. international transfer platforms) this can be reduced to a fraction.

The Turkish programme is a special case: the 400,000 USD citizenship threshold is measured in dollars, but the purchase price flows in lira — the exchange-rate movement can thus affect eligibility too, not just the cost.

The Dubai dirham has been pegged to the dollar (1 USD = 3.6725 AED) since 1997, which is stable — but a Hungarian/euro-based buyer actually takes on USD risk, because the movement of the EUR/USD rate acts on the return through the dirham.

faq.q: Which currency is the safest for buying property abroad?
faq.a: For a Hungarian/euro-based buyer, the eurozone or euro-priced markets (Montenegro, Croatia, Greece, Italy, Spain, Albania) are the most predictable, because there is only one conversion layer. The Dubai dirham is stable (pegged to the dollar) but carries USD exposure.

faq.q: How much can be saved on currency conversion?
faq.a: The bank conversion margin is 1–3%. On a 300,000-euro purchase that is 3,000–9,000 euros. With a dedicated international currency provider the margin can be reduced to a fraction, so for larger sums it is always worth comparing.

faq.q: Why is the Turkish lira risky for the Golden Visa?
faq.a: The citizenship threshold (400,000 USD) is measured in dollars, but the purchase price flows in lira. If the lira weakens between the purchase and the valuation, the property's dollar-measured value can drop and fall below the threshold — which is why currency hedging and timing are critical here.

ingatlanszotar-fogalmak.title: Property glossary: 18 terms to know before buying abroad
ingatlanszotar-fogalmak.description: Freehold, Golden Visa, tapu, escrow, off-plan, NIE — the most important technical terms of buying property abroad explained briefly and clearly.
ingatlanszotar-fogalmak.answer: Buying property abroad is full of technical terms whose misunderstanding costs money. The most important: freehold is full (perpetual) ownership, leasehold is a long lease right; the Golden Visa is RESIDENCE granted for investment, whereas CBI (citizenship by investment) is CITIZENSHIP; escrow is the deposit account that protects the deposit; off-plan is a property still under construction; due diligence is the legal vetting. This glossary explains 18 terms.

sections.h: Ownership and law
p: Freehold — full, time-unlimited ownership right (the equivalent of Hungarian "tulajdon").
Leasehold — a long-term lease/use right (e.g. 25–99 years); not ownership but a fixed-term right. In Bali and Thailand foreigners typically acquire this.
Usufruct — the right to use the property and take its fruits, without transfer of ownership.
Hak Pakai / Hak Sewa / Hak Guna Bangunan — the Indonesian use, lease and building rights that a foreigner can acquire instead of freehold.
Cadastre / title deed — the official property register; ownership becomes complete once registered here. In Turkey this is the "tapu".

sections.h: Immigration and investment
p: Golden Visa — a RESIDENCE permit granted for investment (typically for property purchase); not citizenship and not a passport.
CBI (citizenship by investment) — CITIZENSHIP and a passport granted for investment; with real estate this is offered today primarily by Turkey.
Due diligence — legal vetting: checking the title deed, encumbrances, permits and floor area before the purchase.

sections.h: Purchase process
p: Escrow / deposit account — a neutral (lawyer's or bank) account that holds the deposit until the conditions are met; protects against fraud, especially in off-plan purchases.
Off-plan — a property still under construction, bought on paper; it may be cheaper, but carries developer and completion risk.
Preliminary contract — the contract preparing the final sale, typically with a 10% deposit.
Snagging — the pre-handover defect list (deficiencies to be fixed by the developer in a new build).
Service charge — the condominium common cost (can be a significant item in Dubai and Thailand).

sections.h: Tax and finance
p: Transfer / acquisition tax — the tax payable on the transfer of ownership (e.g. the Turkish "tapu" duty, the Spanish ITP).
ITP / AJD — the Spanish transfer tax (ITP) and the stamp duty (AJD).
Prezzo-valore — the Italian mechanism whereby, between private individuals, the tax base is the (typically lower) cadastral value, not the purchase price.
NIE / codice fiscale / AFM — the Spanish, Italian and Greek foreign tax identifier, without which you cannot contract.
LTV (loan-to-value) — the ratio of the loan to the property value; for a foreign buyer, banks typically grant a lower LTV.

faq.q: What is the difference between freehold and leasehold?
faq.a: Freehold is full, time-unlimited ownership. Leasehold is a fixed-term (typically 25–99 year) lease or use right, which at the end of the term expires or must be renewed. In Bali and Thailand foreigners can often only acquire leasehold — in which case the renewal condition is the most important point to check.

faq.q: Is the Golden Visa the same as citizenship?
faq.a: No. The Golden Visa is a residence permit granted for investment — you can live and come and go in the given country, but your passport does not change. Citizenship (CBI) gives a real passport. The two are often confused; with real estate, citizenship is offered today primarily by Turkey.

faq.q: What is escrow, and why is it important?
faq.a: Escrow is a neutral deposit account (lawyer's or bank) that holds the deposit or the purchase price until the contractual conditions are met. It protects against fraud and developer non-performance, which is why it is especially crucial in off-plan (still-under-construction) purchases that the money goes into escrow, not directly to the seller.
